text: Hello, today we're presenting a case of bioreflux following SADES and its surgical treatment.
This is a fifty-eight-year-old female who underwent a SADES who had a BMI of fifty-one preoperatively.

Five months later she underwent a hiatal hernia repair for long-standing GERD and four months after that she presented to the clinic with nocturnal regurgitation, abdominal pain, and non-responsive to proton pump inhibitors.

The upper GI series showed no hiatal hernia contrast went in the afferent limb and there was a partial bowel obstruction of the stomach and with the fluid all going to the afferent limb we elected to go to surgery.

During the surgery, the surgeon observes that the afferent limb is positioned dependently, causing food to flow into it instead of the efferent limb. A partial obstruction is noted at the outflow from the duodenal ileostomy to the efferent limb.
The surgeon examines the efferent and afferent limbs and proceeds to take down the adhesions separating these two sections.
The surgeon uses a ligature device to address the anatomical issues identified in the duodenal switch procedure, focusing on the afferent and efferent limbs.
The surgeon places anti-obstruction stitches to reorient the duodenal ileostomy, aiming to facilitate bile flow from the afferent limb to the efferent limb and remove the obstruction.
This is what we saw on our upper GI series preoperatively. So this should, in theory, help the bile go past the pylorus and down into the efferent limb without difficulty.
The surgeon replaces the anti-obstruction stitches in the duodenal ileostomy area, as the previous stitches have not held and are no longer visible.
The surgeon identifies a small piece of silk suture that has pulled out and replaces it with two simple interrupted silk sutures to adjust the orientation of the afferent limb and the anastomosis.
The surgeon prepares to create an enteroenterostomy, positioned fifteen centimeters below the duodenal ileostomy, to ensure bile bypasses the duodenal ileostomy and prevents reflux into the stomach.

The surgeon uses electrocautery to make an enterotomy on the afferent limb, followed by another enterostomy on the efferent limb, ensuring the incision is made on the inside to avoid the anti-mesenteric border and leave ample space.
The surgeon uses a GIA stapling device to close the enterotomy defect after mating the two sides together, ensuring a secure closure of the anastomosis.
The surgeon trims a section of the small bowel by gently pulling on it, aiming to preserve as much of the bowel as possible.
The surgeon displays the wide-open configuration of the newly created enteroenterostomy, showing minimal blood loss of five cc's during the procedure, which was completed in eighteen minutes.
The patient stayed one day only for observation and they were discharged home with no further complaints immediately after surgery.
